item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Love the MST3K reference. Very well constructed criticism. I have to agree. There is a germ of a great SCP in there somewhere, I feel, but this fails to realise it. I just can't see how it is anomalous, or dangerous enough to be an SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not a fan of this one. As has been said, the primary anomaly is shaky, not striking me as particularly anomalous. The human brain is a weird thing, dissociative identity/split consciousness isn't exactly that far out there.

Beyond that, this feels like schlock B-movie level horror - and schlock B-movie horror isn't scary at all. It is neither immersive or gripping, two key components to latching onto that horror sense, and the actual concept, which might have some disturbing implications if taken the right direction, is rendered uncompelling by the theatrical presentation (I wouldn't think it would be possible to accuse a written piece of 'overacting,' but here we are). Reading it, I wanted to sit with two robots and make fun of it. The opening doesn't help it, either - it's in line with the rest of the article in being too over-the-top to take seriously.

Other little things, like the constant redactions (which feel unnecessary because we're not given enough information to get a sense of what might be behind them) and the removed interviewer name (why? What purpose would that even serve?) compound the main issues. We're clearly supposed to be led to think something at the end, but the conveyance of that is far insufficient. Just not really working all the way around.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I can't figure it out

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: CGP Grey already spoiled this concept to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A fascinating concept, and I also enjoy the throwback of the dual-class.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While the article has several technical problems that make it difficult to enjoy, my main problem with this is that it really doesn't seem anomalous. Sure, the base idea of a secondary consciousness living inside your brain sounds weird and spooky, but this type of thing is a documented occurrence. The concept of secondary independent personalities inhabiting the same brain as the main consciousness is as old as language itself, there are tonnes of existing diagnosed cases of people who seem to have multiple consciousnesses and there exist entire communities based around inducing this phenomenon deliberately, and very rarely are these manifestations ever hostile toward the main consciousness.

And even if the containment procedures are, as I presume is the case, just a way for the SCP-affected doctor to free his kind, how come nobody has uncovered this yet? At the very least, the O5 council should know of these containment procedures and realize there is no reason for anyone to be subjected to these procedures so long as they are amneticized, seeing as the SCP-3249 instance only activates once the SCP-3259-B criteria are fulfilled and someone can't spread the SCP-3249-A infohazard if they have no memory of the effect.

All in all I'm finding it very difficult to suspend my disbelief here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I take it that the researcher is already affected by this? I only realized that after reading the discussion. Maybe make it more clear in the description that anyone who knows anything about it will be affected, or foreshadow it by making the researcher's limb spasm or something. Honestly, since the end didn't make the dots connect for me, it felt anticlimactic, so no vote from me, sorry.
Small nitpick: "Hello █████████." using D-Class's real names isn't clinical, but that's not really a big deal here imo.

item.title: This is a test
item.content:encoded: This is a test

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The Euclid/Keter is the split opinion of the two personalities of the person writing the article: The dominant one thought it was Keter, because their mind had been overwritten, but it was a fact of the other consciousness's existence, so they thought it was only Euclid.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I want to love it for the effort put into it, and for how engaging it is, but ultimately, the writing needs to be tightened up, and the underlying story needs to be expanded upon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "There is no canon" just means that there is no over arching canon that needs to be followed between individual articles. Clearly, in this article's canon the Ethics Committee exists and has a decent amount of power; enough power to have their own classification level and to be able to redact any information to that classification level.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So basically it's the "Jekyll and Hyde" experiment except Hyde was present and cognizant the entire time up until they were "freed".

Also, I got the impression the binding contract-like containment procedure is more on account of the expunged lead researcher's personal abuse of power rather than anything actually related to containing the phenomenon itself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While true, that seems like it should make it a revolution in neuroscience and psychology, rather than being anomalous. I simply don't find the notion of a parallel consciousness to be anomalous in and of itself. There are a few parasite SCPs I have similar issue with.

Also, how do we know that these really are parallel consciousnesses, rather than a bizarre form of dissociation?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This comment made me re-evaluate some of my understanding of this skip. In particular, just what is the goal of that shadowy Ethics Committee?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1 for sure.

Unsettling in a creeping way, in a way that kept mounting until it reached a stomach-achy dread in me.

Overall, the entry is very long, leading to a feeling of wordiness even though I couldn't identify particular substantial areas to cut.

I didn't see the twist coming and it definitely made me murmur "oh shit," even though in retrospect there isn't much to piece together after the reveal - no sense of a plot or series of connections to make among hints elsewhere, other than "the world isn't what it seems." So I'm echoing some of thedeadlymoose's comments. So that last line definitely added for me, but it wasn't the main driver of my vote, or the feeling of dread. I just hate the idea of 7.3+ billion trapped consciousnesses. Makes me shudder even now.

As others are saying, the dual classification is a bit odd. I do think it's reasonable that activating the consciousnesses themselves, en masse, is a Keter-level threat.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The writing is all-around fine. The concept has been done a lot, but this particular execution of it feels a bit fresh — it's at least written a little differently from what I'd expect, at least at first. It spoils that a bit when we're actually informed what the experience is like — it's every decently-written alien parasite mind-control thing ever, but without anything else there, at least explicitly, since you don't tell us what the ending twist actually is.

(Put another way: I loved the Yeerks in Animorphs, which this reminded me strongly of, but I wouldn't like them if they were written up as an SCP without any of the surrounding story or narrative).

Assorted further issues:

  • No reason for split object class except to bring back a really annoying lazy writing trope ("I can't decide what object class, so I'll pick two!"). Not that I'd downvote for that, but it starts the SCP off on a bad foot for me.
  • Hinges on a layperson's misunderstanding of brain science to work — I see this has been brought up already, though.
  • The interview dialogue is silly and doesn't read like a real person talking. Worst in the first log, but it applies to the rest, too.
  • The not-twist at the end falls flat for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's good, and I give it creepy. But you've gone overboard with the redactions.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I wrote this big long post, where I explained all my confusions. Most of which were answered by re-reading the Skip about 3 times, carefully. I am a jerk, because I really appreciate articles that require only 1 reading to really fully understand, and then subsequent readings turn up the scare/creep/strange factor.

Basically, all of my concerns stem from this idea: I was constantly asking myself "but, this doesn't seem like containment, this seems like thebadguys has successfully found a way to make it appear as though the infohazard is being contained while simultaneously recruiting massive amounts of Foundation and non-Foundation personnel for what I imagine to be a takeovertheworld plot. But that seems so obvious to me, that I then question how this gets past O-5, or… like… anyone. The Foundation has programs, I am certain with which to notice that someone is slowly amassing people from its other sectors for its horrible end game.

If that was the spook that was supposed to come out of this (the idea that this SCP article was CLEARLY written by someone infected and controlled and who IS an instance of the SCP) then I think it would help to revisit and really tighten up the containment procedures. If O-5 or anyone higher up or in any of the counter-memetics divisions got caught up in all this, before they went down they'd open this program wide open as a danger to the stability of the Foundation.

I think I will probably end up upvoting this after the contest, because I love the concept and it isn't poorly executed. But there are some nagging questions and suppositions on my behalf that make it hard to call it SCP-3000

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't understand the split object class here.

(I also don't like "basilisk" here but that's only because the puddingverse, such as there is one, uses that codename for something else, so it is hardly a fair criticism)

Overall I think this dances near where it should be when it talks about the secondary consciousness feeling trapped in a body they don't control, but it never really lands there and has a lot of cruft (like the security porn at the beginning) that contributes nothing.

Novote, I think.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm not sure what exactly I'm meant to take from the end of the interview,

+ My read
– hide block

The researcher is already affected.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have my own other reasons as to why I am downvoting this (until fixed), but I personally think that it should be an author's responsibility to find out the errors in and out of revising.

Sure it is, for the most part, but people are human, and this isn't exactly professional-grade writing most of the time.

This isn't remotely helpful. Could anything be pointed out, perhaps? Simply saying there's problems that need fixing, but not even touching on what kind of problems those are, is pointlessly vague.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wayyy too many "REDACTED" or classified information pieces. It kind of ruins the potential of understanding what SCP this is, and what type of stuff it can truly be capable of.

I have my own other reasons as to why I am downvoting this (until fixed), but I personally think that it should be an author's responsibility to find out the errors in and out of revising.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was with this initially—oh cool, there's a huge disclaimer/contract at the beginning, so it must be winding up to something big; alright, if you know about it then it gets you, that makes sense.

But the horror doesn't grab me at all, because it feels like the hook is that the infohazard gives you an anomalous dissociative disorder. I don't find anything personally horrifying in the concept of "what if there was another consciousness inside your own brain", in large part because that is a real thing that happens to people. And those people adjust to this in individual ways, and feel a variety of emotions about this experience. I feel similarly about the split-brain concept, which I can't comfortably explain away with just "oh they're operating on outdated science".

In the second interview log, it feels like the article veered towards something potentially very horrifying: being "locked" inside someone else's brain while they perform horrible acts that you're forced to feel positively about. But it doesn't feel like the reader is supposed to sympathize with this, which I think really weakens the article. I want to sympathize with the 3000 consciousness, and I briefly thought that the reader was being set up to go in that direction, with the attention given to the Foundation's actions and the psychosurgery… and then the interview logs end on a confusing note, with expungement that I don't understand the purpose of, and I'm left feeling like maybe I was supposed to be on the side of the Foundation all along. If the horror is supposed to be "the Foundation does terrible things to people in the name of experimentation and containment", then it doesn't feel to me like that came through.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If I'm reading this correctly, this is how they make D-class. An entirely new personality, compliant and relatively content with the promise of overcoming the old personality. Individuals completely loyal to the Foundation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
yeah. As a neuroscience person, this was my main issue with it.

Brains are freaky enough on their own when they're damaged. It didn't top the disorders that exist in real life.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: While there are some freaky real-life effects of brain-splitting, it's not literally there being two parallel consciousnesses, not then and not before. As a matter of fact, the whole "left-brained/right-brained" is kinda not actually true, as brain activity is way more complex than that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hesitant upvote. I like the base idea here, but agree with most of the criticism here: the logs are heavy handed and don't add much, and the expungement does more to annoy than enhance the experience of the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The Foundation is not benign. If by torturing or killing condemned criminals they can gather information on how to prevent an infohazard, they will. Remember that D-Class are people who would already have been on death row, and that the interview log confirms that this man is a murderer. As for the Ethics Committee, it may or may not exist/have any power. There is no canon.

It is also definitely anomalous. It denies all current understanding of both the biology of the brain and the transfer of information. An entire secondary consciousness, locked up in your head, forced to feel what you feel? Sounds pretty goddamn anomalous to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The ending felt like a tease. I actually really liked the article up until the end. The ending, on the other hand, just destroyed the entire article to me. You just end it without warning, when I thought we were going to have a great, horrifying story, you end it. You get me invested into something, and you just end it with an unsatisfying conclusion.

-1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think the basic idea of another "you" living in your head is creepy enough that it shouldn't take much of a twist to become horrifying. This article is way too heavy on the narm to be effective. All the extra "this is scary" bits just pull focus from the one part which might actually be scary.

Somewhere in the multiverse there's an article on this topic which is so subtle that it causes insomnia because readers can't quite convince themselves that it's not real. I'd like to read that article. This one, not so much.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: -1. Doesn't give nearly enough info to be creepy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Neat idea, though I swear I've seen something before. (I recall something having to do with alien limb syndrome, could be wrong.) Mostly, the logs are a severe low point, overdramatic and completely undercutting any potential horror.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why is this anomalous?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I actually enjoyed this a lot. But I think what this suffered from (and what I said before) is that it's not immersive enough to give the proper impact.

Still, I will upvote this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a bit brutal and heavy-handed on the split brain phenomenon. It's not a winner, but it doesn't deserve negatives.

Have a +1. I'm unsettled enough by this to want to see it on the main list. You wrote a big check with that opening 'contract'. And you cashed it. That alone deserves the vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
How would the Ethics Committee allow these people to basically just tear people's minds in two for fun? It makes no sense as an SCP, as it is not anomalous, and it makes no sense for the Foundation to do this, as this sort of thing would be shut down pretty quickly and the insane researcher killed.

If there is actually a reason, then I'd like to know, because as it stands it just leaves me confused. This seems like an experiment that would be conducted by nazi germany, not the relatively benign foundation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked this as a draft, but reading it then, I never felt a sense of dread or fear, and was not expecting to upvote this. However, the work you put into those logs was juuust enough to give me a very satisfying 'oh shit' moment. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I gotta agree with what people have said earlier. The expungements are pretty distracting, and the base effect isn't really that interesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Going to have to echo ARD and MrWrong here in that the logs were not particularly creepy for me as a reader, and that the expungements here hinder the article rather than provide a sense of mystery about what is behind the redactions and blackboxes.

On top of that, the base anomaly isn't particularly interesting for me, and as a result is not the strongest source from which to grow the horror of the rest of the article.

-1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Heeeeeey, you changed that last interview up from the last time I saw it! I like what the new direction you took! Even better than what I saw before! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A lot of the writing is substantially improved since the draft I read, particularly the Containment Procedures and the opening of the Description. I'm not sure what exactly I'm meant to take from the end of the interview, but for me the Foundation is source of enough horror here to upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Implications at the end gave me tingles. +1

(Also the science is better now, which I appreciate :) )

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Neat idea, but I did not find it particularly creepy. I do agree that the expungements are not working out for me here.

Serviceable entry, but not what I envision as among the top three in my opinion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not clicking for me. The logs aren't particularly creepy, sound vaguely like every other malicious hidden anomalous group in existence, and I have no idea what I'm supposed to be getting from [DATA EXPUNGED] the Interviewer.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Object Class: Euclid/Keter

my only nitpick is that I don't like the duel class here.

Other than that, I like it, and I think it was done well. Pretty spooky. +1

item.title: Trust nobody. Not even yourself.
item.content:encoded:
Original image is from this page, and was released under Creative Commons Attribution 2.0 Generic (CC BY 2.0), which allows for the image to be shared and adapted as long as appropriate credit is given, and the changes made are pointed out - In this case, the edit I made to the image was the addition of the phrase 'Salutant vos omnes nos'.

Now, thank you to: LordStonefish, Aiden Eldritch, Lazar, minmin, Shaggydredlocks, Dr Leonard, Gog33, DrChandra, Petrograd, psul, Reddit users Old_Gen_Richtofen and brobafett42, as well as all the other users who I am unfortunately unable to recall.

I also want to acknowledge Dr Carrion and Zmax15 - we all (originally) came up with very similar ideas (due to similar/same inspiration/sources), and I hope that no ill will comes between us because of this.

And, of course, good luck everyone on the 3000 contest. pistachio nuts
